title: The Operations Audit Checklist for Manufacturing Companies Under 200 Employees
subtitle: A 15-point walkthrough to find where your floor, office, and shipping are bleeding time and money before you buy a single tool

sections.title: Order Intake and Job Routing
sections.description: Where most manufacturers quietly lose hours. Audit how orders arrive, get logged, and reach the floor before any work starts.

items.text: Consolidate every order into one consistent intake channel instead of email, phone, text, and walk-ins
items.tip: Pick one system of record and route all other channels into it within 24 hours

items.text: Log every order in one system before any production work begins
items.tip: Kill verbal jobs and sticky-note orders, even for your top 3 customers

items.text: Route jobs to departments, machines, or teams using documented rules, not tribal knowledge
items.tip: Write the routing logic down so it survives a vacation or a resignation

items.text: Document how many times a single order is manually re-entered or copied between systems
items.tip: Shadow one order end to end and count every retype, export, and paste

sections.title: Production Tracking and Floor Visibility
sections.description: If you cannot see the floor in real time, you are managing by gut feel. Confirm you have live signals, not end-of-shift guesses.

items.text: Display a live board showing jobs in progress, queued, and blocked
items.tip: A shop-floor TV pulling from your system beats a whiteboard that lies by lunch

items.text: Require operators to log job status changes as they happen, not at end of shift
items.tip: Put a tablet or scanner at each workstation so logging takes under 10 seconds

items.text: Track every downtime event with a reason code, not a vague time gap
items.tip: Start with 8 reason codes max so operators actually use them

items.text: Pull an actual vs estimated production time report for any job in the last 30 days on demand
items.tip: If it takes more than 5 minutes or 3 people, your data is not trustworthy yet

sections.title: Data and Systems Integration
sections.description: Disconnected tools are the silent tax on every small manufacturer. Map where data lives and how it moves before you automate anything.

items.text: List every system touching an order from quote to cash in one document
items.tip: Include spreadsheets and shared drives, they count as systems

items.text: Designate a single source of truth for customers, parts, and inventory
items.tip: Every other system should read from it, not hold its own version

items.text: Map which systems talk to each other automatically and which require human copy-paste
items.tip: Circle the manual handoffs in red, those are your first automation targets

sections.title: Quality Control and Shipping
sections.description: The last mile is where margin quietly disappears through rework, chargebacks, and missed ship dates.

items.text: Define QC checkpoints with pass/fail criteria documented for every product line
items.tip: If QC lives in one inspector's head, you do not have QC, you have a dependency

items.text: Track rework and scrap by job, reason, and dollar cost every week
items.tip: Review the top 3 reasons monthly with the floor lead, not just the controller

items.text: Report on-time ship rate against promised date, not rescheduled date
items.tip: Rescheduling to hit 100% is how you lose customers quietly

items.text: Confirm shipping receives job completion and packing data without manual re-entry
items.tip: A printed traveler retyped into ShipStation is a bug, not a workflow

completion_cta.headline: You have the map. Now fix the right things first.
completion_cta.description: Take your flagged items, rank them by dollar impact, and tackle the top 3 before you buy any software. If you want a second set of eyes on the audit, book a call and we will walk through it together.
